locked_facts: A transistor has three doped regions: emitter (heavily doped, moderate size), base (lightly doped, very thin ~μm), and collector (moderately doped, largest area)
For active mode operation: emitter-base junction is forward biased, collector-base junction is reverse biased
Kirchhoff's current rule at the transistor: I_E = I_B + I_C, where I_E is the largest current
Current gain β (common emitter) = I_C / I_B, typically 20–200; α (common base) = I_C / I_E, typically 0.95–0.99
The base is made very thin and lightly doped so that most emitter-injected carriers (>95%) reach the collector instead of recombining in the base
In NPN transistor: electrons are majority carriers flowing from emitter to collector through the thin base; in PNP: holes flow from emitter to collector
Transistor as amplifier: small change in base current (ΔI_B) produces large change in collector current (ΔI_C = β × ΔI_B)
truth_statements: The emitter-base junction must be forward biased and the collector-base junction must be reverse biased for the transistor to operate in the active (amplifying) region
The thin and lightly doped base ensures that most majority carriers injected from the emitter diffuse across to the collector without recombining, giving high current gain
I_E = I_B + I_C holds at all times by Kirchhoff's current law, with I_B being only a small fraction (1–5%) of I_E
The output characteristics (I_C vs V_CE at constant I_B) show three regions: saturation, active, and cutoff, with the active region used for amplification
Voltage gain in common emitter configuration = β × (R_load / R_input), allowing a small input signal to produce a large output signal

aha_moment.what_to_show: A tiny change in base current (slider moves I_B from 20 μA to 40 μA) causes I_C to jump from 2 mA to 4 mA — the collector ammeter reading changes 100× more than the base ammeter. Electrons are shown flooding from emitter through thin base into collector with only a few recombining in the base.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Split ammeter display: base ammeter in μA barely moves while collector ammeter in mA shows dramatic change, with β value updating in real time
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that the base does not supply the collector current — it merely controls how much emitter current reaches the collector, like a valve controlling a large water flow

simulation_states.label: Transistor structure
simulation_states.description: An NPN transistor shown with three labeled regions: emitter (E, heavily doped), base (B, thin and lightly doped), and collector (C, moderately doped, largest). Two junctions are marked: emitter-base (EB) and collector-base (CB). No external voltage applied yet. Carrier concentrations shown with dot density: many electrons in E and C, few in B.
simulation_states.emphasis: Understand the three regions and why the base is thin and lightly doped

simulation_states.label: Biasing applied — active mode
simulation_states.description: V_BB forward biases the EB junction; V_CC reverse biases the CB junction. Electrons are injected from emitter into base. Due to thin base, ~95% of electrons diffuse across to collector (shown as electron stream crossing base). ~5% recombine in base (shown as a few electrons disappearing). Three ammeter readings appear: I_E, I_B (small), I_C (large). Arrow shows I_E = I_B + I_C.
simulation_states.emphasis: Most emitter electrons reach collector — base current is tiny because base is thin and lightly doped

simulation_states.label: Current amplification demo
simulation_states.description: I_B is increased slightly (e.g., 20 μA → 40 μA). I_C responds with a proportionally larger increase (2 mA → 4 mA). β = I_C/I_B is displayed and stays constant. The right panel shows the output characteristic curve (I_C vs V_CE) with the operating point moving upward as I_B increases. The amplification factor is highlighted.
simulation_states.emphasis: Small ΔI_B causes large ΔI_C — this is transistor amplification with gain β

simulation_states.label: Interactive transistor lab
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts V_BB (controls I_B) and V_CC using sliders. All three currents update in real time. The right panel plots I_C vs V_CE characteristic curves for different I_B values. Saturation, active, and cutoff regions are color-coded on the graph. A toggle switches between NPN and PNP (carrier directions reverse). β value displayed continuously.
simulation_states.emphasis: Explore how base current controls collector current — find saturation, active, and cutoff regions on the I-V curve

common_misconceptions.belief: The base current provides or creates the collector current
trigger_phrases: base se collector current aata hai
common_misconceptions.correction: The base current does not supply the collector current. The emitter injects carriers, and the base merely controls how many of them reach the collector. I_B is just the small fraction that recombines in the base. Think of I_B as a control valve, not a source.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show electron stream from emitter splitting: 95% going to collector, 5% recombining in base — the base does not generate electrons

common_misconceptions.belief: Both junctions of the transistor must be forward biased for it to work
common_misconceptions.correction: For amplification (active mode), the emitter-base junction must be forward biased and the collector-base junction must be reverse biased. If both are forward biased, the transistor enters saturation (acts as a closed switch, no amplification).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Toggle between active mode (EB forward, CB reverse → amplification) and saturation (both forward → flat I_C, no gain)

common_misconceptions.belief: Emitter and collector are interchangeable since both are the same semiconductor type (n in NPN)
common_misconceptions.correction: The emitter is heavily doped and smaller in area while the collector is moderately doped and has a larger area. Swapping them drastically reduces current gain because the base-collector junction cannot inject carriers efficiently.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show doping density labels: emitter n+ (dark dense dots), collector n (lighter dots), and explain why asymmetric doping matters for gain
